mappings.rationaleAI: Industrial and product design covering form, function, and mass production relates to the nature and scope of decorative arts and functional design (A). Coverage of Nokia, Ford, Sony, and Lego product design exemplifies industrial design as an art (C.2.a) and diverse kinds of industrial design classified by function (C.2.b).
Fashion as design, manufacturing, and cultural communication from haute couture to ready-to-wear directly addresses dress design (C.1) and dress and dress accessories (C.1.a).

Visual presentation of written language covering letterforms, typeface design, and font technology from early printing through digital media relates to handwriting systems and calligraphy (C.4.a) and printing arts including typography and printing design (C.4.b).
Advertising strategy, media channels, and campaign design including print, outdoor, and internet-based formats directly address advertising art and design (C.4.c).
Brand identity construction and the mechanisms of brand recognition connect to the design of signs and symbols used primarily for identification (C.4.d).
History of writing systems from cuneiform and hieroglyphs through alphabetic scripts connects to handwriting systems and styles, calligraphy, and lettering (C.4.a).
Egyptian hieroglyphs as a writing system with pictographic aesthetic and sacral function spanning over three thousand years directly connects to handwriting systems and styles (C.4.a).
Materials science studying metals, ceramics, and polymers connects to decorative objects classified by materials (B), including glass (B.4) and metals (B.5) as materials used in decorative arts.

Architecture as art form spanning from ancient Egypt through modernism connects to interior design's integration with architecture (C.3.b) and interior design as an art (C.3.a) through the relationship between decorative elements and building design.

Modernism as an aesthetic movement transforming art, architecture, and design including Dada and surrealism relates broadly to the nature and scope of the arts of decoration and functional design (A) through its influence on design aesthetics.

Philosophical examination of beauty, taste, and aesthetic experience including everyday experience beyond fine arts relates to the nature and scope of the arts of decoration and functional design (A).
Classical Greek and Roman architectural and sculptural traditions including temple design connect to stone as a decorative material (B.7) through classical architectural ornamentation.
Covers decorative traditions across cultures including body decoration, textiles, and ceremonial objects (C.1.c, B.10).
Prehistoric decorative arts including pottery, jewelry, and body decoration are among the earliest functional design objects (B.1, C.1).
Mineralogy covering crystal structures and mineral families including quartz and pyrite connects to gems as a decorative arts material (B.3).
Covers the chemistry of materials including ceramics, glass, and metals relevant to understanding decorative art media (B.1, B.4, B.5).
Covers Egyptian decorative objects, sculpture, and painting spanning four thousand years, directly relevant to decorative arts using metals, stone, and other materials in the ancient world.
Covers Renaissance decorative arts in their cultural context, including the role of art in homes and civic spaces and patronage systems, relevant to decorative arts and interior design.
